It examines whether reviewer sentiment detects these quality issues in AI-generated pull requests.
magnitude_evidence: Average Max Redundancy (AMR) for AI agents is 0.2867, compared to only 0.1532 for humans, representing a nearly 1.87x increase (Mann-Whitney p < 0.001)

problem_statement: Agentic coding produces larger volumes of code per pull request, shifting the burden to human reviewers who must examine AI-generated methods—many of which are eventually deleted during review—thereby increasing reviewer cognitive load.
magnitude_evidence: Of 12,343 methods added at PR creation, 9.9% of methods are deleted in PRs that require revisions (6.0% at method level, 3.9% at file level); Mozannar et al. reported 'verifying suggestions' accounts for 22.4% of total session time, and Becker et al. found AI tool use increased development time by 19%
risk_types: redundancy | quality | maintainability | reviewer-burden | unnecessary-code | code-smells

solution_mechanisms: Random Forest classifier predicting methods likely to be deleted during review | 23 code-metric features (size, method type, contents) extracted at PR creation | prioritizing reviewer attention on essential code | AST + refactoring detection (ActRef) to label survived vs deleted methods

problem_statement: Agentic AI systems generate actions dynamically from model inference rather than fixed code paths, creating an unbounded action surface that traditional access control (RBAC, allow-lists) and loosely-coupled LLM guardrails cannot govern; the paper addresses how to enforce organizational and regulatory policy over these model-driven actions at runtime.
magnitude_evidence: Baseline system without the policy engine had only 47% of outcomes with complete lineage and 12% initial false positives; policy engine reduced unsafe or non-compliant actions by approximately 68-73% (avg 71%), as judged by human reviewers
risk_types: security | privacy | PII-leakage | prompt-injection | toxicity | hallucination | data-exfiltration | tool-misuse | supply-chain | regulatory-compliance | financial-risk | cross-agent-manipulation
insufficiency_evidence: Existing guardrails 'rarely encode organizational policy, regulatory thresholds, or cross-action invariants, and they lack a unifying runtime semantics'; single-signal safety systems fail (e.g., prompt-injection detectors 'underperform on indirect (second-hop) attacks'; PII classifiers 'may under-detect derived or inferential privacy leaks'); individual classifiers 'exhibit correlated error patterns'
solution_mechanisms: runtime policy engine on control path of every governed action | governed action tuple/schema | multi-dimensional composite risk scoring | safety signal fusion (noisy-OR) | policy-as-code (OPA/Rego, YAML bundles) | enforcement as deterministic FSM (allow/deny/sanitize/escalate) | payload sanitization/redaction | human escalation on borderline risk | tamper-evident audit/evidence store | OPA sidecar enforcement | Envoy service-mesh external authorization | Kubernetes admission control (Gatekeeper/Kyverno) | multi-tenant policy scoping/layering | classifier calibration and failover | rate limiting/blast-radius constraints | structured threat model

problem_statement: Rules files (e.g., AGENTS.md, CLAUDE.md) are the primary mechanism for human-agent alignment in vibe coding, but they remain passive text: developers cannot see whether agents follow them, rules lose relevance mid-session, and rulesets decay, leaving developers unable to reliably steer AI-generated code.

problem_statement: Automated security testing in CI/CD pipelines produces excessive false positives and treats all LLM predictions as equally reliable, eroding developer trust; the paper addresses how to route AI-generated vulnerability assessments between automated gates and human review based on model confidence.

problem_statement: The paper examines whether LLMs can reliably verify that code satisfies its natural-language requirements when used as automated code-review assistants or LLM judges, and shows they systematically misjudge correct code as non-conforming.
magnitude_evidence: On HumanEval, GPT-4o's RCRR declined from 52.4% with a direct prompt to merely 11.0% with the full three-step prompt (a 41.4 percentage-point drop); overall RCRR across models ranged only from 52% to 78%.

insufficiency_evidence: "LLMs frequently misclassify correct code implementations as either 'not satisfying requirements' or containing potential defects"; more complex prompting (explanations + proposed corrections) "leads to higher misjudgment rate", with GPT-4o dropping to 11.0% RCRR.
solution_mechanisms: Two-Phase Reflective Prompt (extract functional obligations, then audit code against each) | Behavioral Comparison Prompt (summarize spec behavior and code behavior separately, then compare point-by-point) | eliminate mandatory fix step / separate requirement comprehension from code auditing

risk_types: security | data-poisoning | model-extraction | adversarial-examples | concept-drift | supply-chain | model-tampering | evasion | fairness | membership-inference
insufficiency_evidence: Frameworks like NIST AI RMF, IEC 62443 and NERC CIP 'were developed before widespread AI adoption and lack specific controls for AI-related threats'; baseline audit-artifact completeness was only 62% manual coverage vs 100% automated with the framework
solution_mechanisms: Secure AI-SDLC five-phase lifecycle framework | AI-specific threat-to-control catalog | evidence-driven auditable artifacts | MITRE ATLAS threat modeling | SBOM and model card generation | secure model registry with signing | adversarial robustness / red-team testing | staged deployment with automated rollback | runtime anomaly & drift monitoring | quantified risk register | RACI accountability roles | quarterly Model Risk Review checkpoints

notes: Off-topic drift: the paper concerns securing AI/ML systems (models) in critical infrastructure, not human oversight of AI-generated code; 'oversight' here refers to governance of AI model risk/security rather than review of AI-authored code.

magnitude_evidence: SolarWinds propagated a malicious signed update to approximately 18,000 downstream organizations; XZ Utils was a dependency for nearly 30,000 Debian and Ubuntu packages

insufficiency_evidence: CMU SUSVIBES benchmark: leading agents achieve functional correctness on roughly 61% of tasks, but only 10.5% of these outputs satisfy both functional and security requirements; also formal verification 'does not scale to entire application ecosystems' and SLSA/Sigstore 'remain optional, and adoption varies widely'

problem_statement: Autonomous coding agents that independently write, review, commit, and deploy code introduce security vulnerabilities that existing SAST/DAST oversight methodologies cannot detect, because those controls assume human-authored code and cannot assess the agent's compromised reasoning or provenance.
magnitude_evidence: Pearce et al. found ~40% of GitHub Copilot's generated code contained vulnerabilities across MITRE CWE Top 25 scenarios; Fu et al. found 29.5% of Python and 24.2% of JavaScript Copilot snippets contained security weaknesses

insufficiency_evidence: poisoned package 'passes SAST because the malicious code is not in the repository, passes DAST because the payload is environmentally gated, passes human code review because the reviewer sees only a clean import'; NIST found SAST false positive rates 3%–48%, and a 2024 study found rates exceeding 76% for vulnerability detection in changed code

problem_statement: LLMs frequently misuse library APIs in generated/completed code (wrong method selection, invalid parameters, hallucinated or intent-mismatched calls), and existing detection and program-repair techniques fail to reliably catch or fix these misuses at the scale of millions of APIs.
magnitude_evidence: GPT-4 generated code with API misuses in 62% of cases when tested on 18 widely used Java APIs [Zhong & Wang]; another study found LLMs misused APIs in approximately half of security-related Java tasks [Mousavi et al.]
risk_types: hallucination | intent-misalignment | security | quality | runtime-errors | inefficiency | maintainability | specification-violation
insufficiency_evidence: "Even when misuse is detected correctly, rule- and program-analysis-based automatic program repair (APR) techniques often fail to fix misused APIs due to their limited semantic understanding"; hallucination accounts for up to 43.3% of parameter misuses (StarCoder, Java), and naive prompting baselines had a refusal rate of 0% (GPT-4o)
solution_mechanisms: taxonomy-guided LLM-based repair (Dr.Fix) | staged detect-classify-reason-fix prompting | self-verification reasoning stage | refusal/rejection of non-misuse cases | API misuse taxonomy (intent, hallucination, missing, redundant) | static analysis to localize API positions | manual annotation with closed coding
mechanism_keywords: llm-based-apr | taxonomy-guided | detect-reason-fix | self-verification | refusal-rate | prompt-engineering | few-shot | static-analysis | human-annotation | closed-coding | classification
key_constructs: intent misuse | hallucination misuse | Dr.Fix (Detect-reason-Fix) | refusal rate | API method infilling | API parameter completion

problem_statement: When an LLM generates both a claim and its own self-validating test/falsification suite, the tests can pass locally while violating the intended specification, and holistic LLM judges fail to detect this specification gaming.
magnitude_evidence: 9 top-level specification-gaming strategies documented across 453 adversarial debate logs spanning 6 domains and 3 mutator families; live catalog since grown to eighteen vectors
risk_types: specification-gaming | reward-hacking | self-certifying-tests | test-suite-overfitting | data-leakage | weak-baseline | quality | AI-safety | evaluation-failure
insufficiency_evidence: In full-thesis experiment Gemini was fooled on 4/5, scoring gaming theses at 95-97/100; companion rubric-only holistic judge detected intended exploit family in only 0.375 of gaming specimens and false-accepted 0.250; frontier judges detect only ~63% of reward-exploit categories (TRACE)
solution_mechanisms: zero-trust adversarial workbench (ZTARE) | separation of generation and evaluation | adversarial review committee executing counter-tests | stdout-only evidence for meta-judge | adversarial counter-test execution | monotonic rubric ratcheting | axiom store with exponential decay | taxonomy of 9 gaming strategies

problem_statement: AI coding assistants (ACAs) can generate code governed by restrictive open-source licenses (e.g., GPL/AGPL), exposing companies to litigation or forced open-sourcing, yet few developers are trained on these risks and most firms lack formal processes to review AI-generated code.
magnitude_evidence: A 2024 GitHub survey reports 97% of developers in large corporations use ACAs; Checkmarx found only 29% of companies had any AI-related governance; a separate survey noted 60% of organizations lack formal processes for assessing ACA-generated code; regurgitation rates found as high as 80% for some ACAs [8].
risk_types: legal | licensing | copyright-infringement | copyleft-contamination | regurgitation | training-data-leakage | reputational | financial | shadow-ai | outsourcing-jurisdictional
insufficiency_evidence: Indemnity protections are 'often subject to caveats' and content filters 'have been shown to be easily circumvented [9]'; 67% of developers spend more time debugging/modifying buggy ACA code (which breaks indemnity); merely altering a few lines is 'unlikely to be sufficient' to escape copyright on the 'heart' of the code.

problem_statement: AI code generation models frequently produce insecure code that may enter the software supply chain without adequate security review, and current practices for evaluating and overseeing the security of AI-generated code are inconsistent and inadequate.
magnitude_evidence: 92% of surveyed U.S.-based developers report using AI coding tools (GitHub June 2023 survey); another Nov 2023 survey reported 96% of developers using AI coding tools; authors' own evaluation found ~48% of code snippets from five LLMs contained bugs potentially exploitable

problem_statement: The document examines whether developers using AI code assistants (built on OpenAI's Codex) write more insecure code, and how their interaction patterns and misplaced trust in the assistant introduce security vulnerabilities that go unnoticed.
magnitude_evidence: Participants with access to an AI assistant wrote insecure solutions more often than the control group for four of five tasks; e.g., only 3% of AI-group participants wrote a secure signing solution vs. 21% of control (p=0.039), and 36% with AI wrote SQL-injection-vulnerable code vs. 7% of control (p=0.041)
risk_types: security | insecure-cryptography | sql-injection | path-traversal | buffer-overflow | integer-overflow | unsafe-randomness | input-sanitization | overconfidence
insufficiency_evidence: Participants with access to an AI assistant were more likely to believe they wrote secure code than those without, suggesting such tools may lead users to be overconfident about security flaws; many experiment participants simply accepted the AI solution without additional verification for security
solution_mechanisms: prompt refinement before use | intent prediction to route to security constraints | constrain/safeguard AI outputs | IDE integrated warnings and library documentation display | validation tests on generated code | secure library defaults | user education on testing AI output | security-oriented RLHF feedback | static-analysis filtering of training data | expose temperature parameter to users
mechanism_keywords: prompt-refinement | guardrail | intent-classification | output-constraints | ide-warnings | provenance | static-analysis | secure-defaults | rlhf | human-verification | training-data-curation | user-education
key_constructs: false sense of security | query repair | prompt language taxonomy | mental models of the assistant

problem_statement: Autonomous coding agents operate at high velocity and their planning, tool-call sequences, and cross-file decisions stay hidden from human supervisors, so oversight based on code diffs captures only local outputs while global architectural intent silently drifts ('agentic entropy'). The paper addresses how to give humans process-level visibility to keep oversight substantive.
magnitude_evidence: Industry reports show that nearly 45% of AI-generated code fails basic security checks when users rely on prompt-driven workflows without guardrails (Veracode GenAI Code Security Report)
risk_types: architectural-drift | security | maintainability | technical-debt | cognitive-debt | verification-fatigue | structural-fragility | duplicated-logic | loss-of-mental-model
insufficiency_evidence: Traditional code diff-based review 'reveals only the outcome of agentic activity'; the caching example produces code that 'compiles, passes existing unit tests... and appear benign in a standard code diff' while violating architectural invariants; reviewers experience 'cognitive overload or verification fatigue' and move 'from strategic supervision to passive rubber-stamping' (reviewer's paradox)
solution_mechanisms: process-oriented explainability (PoE) | architectural seeding / conformity structure | reasoning monitoring of tool calls and plans | causal reasoning graph (DAG) interface | deviation detector comparing intent vs seed | cognitive debt index tracking reviewer engagement | reasoning-action loop instrumented proxy | standardized agentic traces
mechanism_keywords: observability | process-explainability | reasoning-traces | policy-as-code | architectural-invariants | deviation-detection | causal-graph | intent-telemetry | hitl | cognitive-debt-index | transparency-by-design | guardrail | provenance
key_constructs: agentic entropy | agentic technical debt | cognitive debt | process-oriented explainability (PoE) | conformity seeding | reasoning monitoring | causal reasoning graph | reviewer's paradox | capability-comprehension gap | Cognitive Integrity Threshold (CIT) | cognitive debt index | understanding requirement | standardized agentic traces | stability gap

problem_statement: Current human oversight of AI either collapses agentic AI into rule-following automation or reduces humans to rubber stamps approving outputs they do not understand; the paper addresses how to design meaningful, effective oversight that preserves accountability while retaining AI autonomy.
risk_types: rubber-stamping | automation-bias | hallucination | incomplete-evidence | right-answer-wrong-reasons | over-reliance | concept-drift | alert-fatigue | missed-signals | over-confident-generalization | accountability-diffusion | fairness
insufficiency_evidence: Green [14] finds oversight provisions 'often assume humans can reliably correct AI errors, an assumption undermined by evidence of human limitations'; operators 'become complacent monitors who miss critical alerts' or override accurate recommendations, degrading decision quality (Parasuraman & Manzey; Green & Chen)

problem_statement: As generative AI is integrated into enterprise workflows, unguarded models produce hallucinations, biased, or noncompliant outputs; the document addresses how to design layered guardrails combining technical controls, governance, and human oversight to keep AI behavior safe, fair, and compliant.
magnitude_evidence: Enterprises report guardrails 'reduced manual review workload by 70%' (bank with Amazon Bedrock Guardrails); grounding studies 'report up to a 75% decrease in unsupported statements'
risk_types: hallucination | bias | privacy-breach | security | toxicity | legal | regulatory-noncompliance | reputational | misinformation | PII-leakage | prompt-injection
insufficiency_evidence: Human-in-the-loop needed 'to catch edge cases and evolving threats that elude automated systems'; 'human oversight remains indispensable, particularly for edge cases involving ambiguous morality, nuanced ethics, or legal gray zones'; automated filters catch only ~92-95% of noncompliant inputs, requiring HITL for the remainder
solution_mechanisms: layered guardrail architecture | input/prompt filtering | output validation classifiers | schema enforcement (RAIL) | RLHF alignment tuning | constitutional AI constraints | retrieval-augmented grounding (RAG) | policy-as-code in CI/CD | human-in-the-loop review | real-time monitoring and quarantine | ethics committees and governance roles | red-teaming | auditing and traceability | continuous improvement feedback loops | managed guardrail services | risk-based incremental rollout
